page.content: In the study by Jousset et al., 2004, the objective was to compare RTW in patients participating in a multidisciplinary functional restoration programme to those in active individual therapy, revealing a significantly lower mean number of self-reported sick-leave days, with outcome measures including sick-leave days over two years, RTW within one week, and subjective ratings of ability to work and improved physical condition.

lines.text: In the study by Jousset et al., 2004, the objective was to compare RTW in patients participating in a multidisciplinary functional
restoration programme to those in active individual therapy, revealing a significantly lower mean number of self-reported sick-
leave days, with outcome measures including sick-leave days over two years, RTW within one week, and subjective ratings of
ability to work and improved physical condition.

Joy et al.,2001RTW after work-hardening programmeEither part-time of full-time RTW at the time of follow-up phone calls (in original or alternative job)• Functional capacity• Age• Length of injury (days)• Time in program (days)• Work status (did or did not RTW)• Pain level• Pain tolerance (% improvement)• Activity tolerance (% improvement)
Lambeek et al., 2010to evaluate the effectiveness of an integrated care programme, combining a patient directed and a workplace directed intervention, for patients with chronic low back painDuration of sick leave due to low back pain in calendar days from the day op randomisation until full RTW in own or other work with equal earnings for at least four weeks without recurrence, partial of full.• Primary outcome: duration of time off work (work disability)• Secondary outcome:➢ intensity of pain and functional status➢ the integrated care programme substantially reduced disability due to chronic low back pain in private and working life➢ improvement of pain between groups did not differ significantly

Sullivan et al., 2006Compared percentage of RTW in patients participating in PGAP + PT (6) to those participating in PT (7) aloneReturning to full-time pre-injury employment or alternative employment• RTW (primary outcome variable)• Catastrophizing• Fear of movement or reinjury• Perceived disability• Pain severity

page.content:
Joy et al., 2001 focused on RTW after a work-hardening programme, measuring part-time or full-time RTW status at follow-up, alongside functional capacity, age, injury length (days), program duration (days), work status, pain level, pain tolerance (% improvement), and activity tolerance (% improvement). Lambeek et al., 2010 evaluated an integrated care programme for chronic low back pain, assessing sick leave duration until full RTW, with outcomes on time off work, pain intensity, and functional status. Schene et al., 2007 found significant differences between TAU and TAU + OT regarding RTW timing and total hours worked, measuring depression, work resumption, work stress, service use, and economic evaluation. Sullivan et al., 2006 compared RTW percentages among PGAP + PT versus PT alone, focusing on full-time employment status and various psychological factors. Vanderploeg et al., 2008 examined RTW or school return in two rehabilitation approaches, assessing employment status, life satisfaction, social aspects, and emotional well-being.

In Table 2, the number of Pro.obo Releases shows: Proteins at 79 (1.0), 149 (2.0), 289 (3.0); PRO terms at 667 (1.0), 1023 (2.0), 1269 (3.0); ProEvo terms at 119 (1.0), 223 (2.0), 397 (3.0); and ProForm terms at 548 (1.0), 800 (2.0), 872 (3.0).
